title: Web Search Engine — System Design
description: Design a web search engine: crawl the web, build an inverted index over billions of documents, and answer keyword queries with ranked results in well under a second. The themes are the crawl pipeline, inverted-index construction, document-sharded scatter/gather serving, and ranking.

nodes.label: Crawler Fleet
nodes.description: Fetches web pages, respecting politeness and robots rules.

nodes.label: URL Frontier
nodes.description: Prioritized, politeness-aware queue of URLs to crawl.

nodes.label: Seen-URL / Dedup Store
nodes.description: Tracks crawled/known URLs and content hashes to avoid re-crawl and dupes.

nodes.label: Indexing Pipeline
nodes.description: Parses docs, extracts terms/links, builds inverted-index segments.

nodes.label: Inverted Index Shards
nodes.description: Document-partitioned posting lists; the searchable index.

nodes.label: Link Graph / Page Scores
nodes.description: Web link graph and precomputed query-independent scores (e.g. PageRank).

nodes.label: Query Service
nodes.description: Parses the query and scatters it to index shards (aggregator).

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores candidate docs by relevance + page score.

nodes.label: Doc / Snippet Cache
nodes.description: Hot document metadata and snippets for result hydration.

nodes.label: Searcher
nodes.description: Issues queries and reads ranked results.

functional: Crawl the web continuously and keep the index fresh.
Answer keyword queries with a ranked list of relevant documents.
Return titles, URLs, and snippets for results.
Support multi-term queries and basic operators.
nonFunctional: Query latency p99 well under a second across billions of documents.
Crawl politely (robots.txt, rate limits per host).
Index freshness: new/changed content appears within a bounded time.
Scale storage and serving horizontally over the whole corpus.

capacity.label: Documents
capacity.note: Drives index size and shard count.

capacity.label: Index size
capacity.note: Posting lists across all terms; document-partitioned.

api.description: Run a query and return ranked results.

dataModel.name: documents
dataModel.note: Per-doc metadata and content for snippets/ranking.

patterns.name: Politeness-aware crawl frontier
patterns.what: A prioritized URL queue that respects robots.txt and per-host rate limits.
patterns.whenToUse: Crawling at scale without hammering or getting blocked by sites.

patterns.name: Inverted index
patterns.what: Map each term to the list of documents (and positions) containing it.
patterns.whenToUse: Fast keyword lookup over a large corpus.

patterns.name: Document partitioning (sharding)
patterns.what: Split the index by document so each shard holds full postings for its docs.
patterns.whenToUse: Corpora too large for one machine; enables parallel scatter/gather.

patterns.name: Scatter-gather query
patterns.what: Send the query to all shards, collect top-k from each, merge and re-rank.
patterns.whenToUse: Document-partitioned indexes where any doc can match.

patterns.name: Two-phase ranking
patterns.what: Cheap candidate retrieval per shard, then an expensive re-rank of the merged candidates.
patterns.whenToUse: When full ranking over all matches is too costly.

patterns.name: Result + doc caching
patterns.what: Cache popular query results and hot document snippets.
patterns.whenToUse: Skewed query/document popularity (a few queries dominate).

steps.title: 1. Crawl the Web with a URL Frontier
description: A crawler fleet fetches pages, but you can't just BFS naively: you must be polite (obey robots.txt, rate-limit per host), avoid re-fetching unchanged pages, and prioritize important/fresh URLs.

A URL frontier is a prioritized, politeness-aware queue: it hands out URLs respecting per-host delays and priority. Crawlers fetch, store the raw page, dedup by URL and content hash, and enqueue newly discovered links.
A seen-URL/content store prevents re-crawling the same page and detects duplicate content across URLs.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you crawl billions of pages fast without overwhelming any single website?

concepts.definition: A prioritized, politeness-bounded queue of URLs to crawl.
concepts.whyItMatters: Balances coverage, freshness, and politeness; the heart of a scalable crawler.
concepts.example: Per-host buckets with next_fetch_at delays + priority by importance/freshness.

whyNow: Crawling is the data source; a politeness-aware frontier is what makes large-scale crawling both effective and acceptable to the web.

recap.after: A growing, deduped corpus of fetched pages.
recap.newRisk: Raw pages aren't searchable — they must be indexed.

traps.trap: Naive BFS crawling without per-host limits.
traps.why: You DDoS sites, get blocked, and waste budget re-fetching unchanged pages.
traps.instead: A prioritized frontier with politeness and content-hash dedup.

steps.title: 2. Build the Inverted Index
description: To answer 'which docs contain these words' fast, build an inverted index: for each term, a posting list of the documents (and positions) containing it — the opposite of scanning every document.

The indexing pipeline parses each stored doc, tokenizes/normalizes terms (lowercase, stemming), and writes postings. Indexing is a batch/stream pipeline that produces immutable index segments, periodically merged.
Also extract links to build the link graph used for query-independent scoring.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why an inverted index rather than scanning documents for the query terms?

concepts.definition: term -> list of documents (with positions/frequencies) containing it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a corpus scan into a posting-list lookup and intersection.

whyNow: The inverted index is the core data structure of search; everything in serving operates on its posting lists.

recap.after: Term posting lists + a link graph.
recap.newRisk: The index is far too large for one machine — it must be sharded, and queries must hit all shards.

steps.title: 3. Shard the Index and Scatter-Gather Queries
description: The index spans petabytes, so partition it by document: each shard holds the full inverted index for its slice of the corpus. (Document partitioning beats term partitioning here — each shard answers independently and load is even.)
A query is scattered to all shards in parallel; each returns its top-k candidates for the query, and an aggregator merges them. Replicate each shard for QPS and availability.
This scatter-gather is the latency challenge: total latency is the slowest shard, so you bound per-shard work and use the cheap first-phase scoring to limit candidates.
steps.decisionPrompt: Partition the index by document or by term? What does each do to a multi-term query?

concepts.definition: Each shard holds complete postings for a subset of documents.
concepts.whyItMatters: Every shard answers any query independently; load and latency are balanced.
concepts.example: 10K shards each index ~10M docs; a query fans out to all, top-k from each.

concepts.definition: Fan a query to all shards, collect partial results, and merge.
concepts.whyItMatters: Parallelizes search across the whole corpus within the latency budget.
concepts.example: Aggregator merges 10K x top-100 into a global candidate set.

whyNow: Sharded scatter-gather is how search scales to billions of docs while staying sub-second; the document-vs-term partitioning choice is the key senior decision.

recap.after: Document-sharded, replicated index served via scatter-gather.
recap.newRisk: Merged candidates still need good ranking, and fanning out everything is expensive.

bottlenecks.issue: Tail latency: the slowest shard sets query latency.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Replicas + hedged requests; cap per-shard work; skip slow shards with partial results.

steps.title: 4. Rank Results
description: Relevance is more than 'contains the words'. Use a two-phase approach: each shard cheaply retrieves and scores candidates (e.g. TF-IDF/BM25 text relevance), then the aggregator re-ranks the merged candidate set with a richer model.

Blend signals: text relevance, query-independent page score (link-graph authority like PageRank), freshness, and quality. The expensive re-rank runs only on the small merged candidate set, not the whole corpus.
Query-independent scores (page authority) are precomputed offline from the link graph so they're a cheap lookup at query time.
steps.decisionPrompt: You have 100K matching docs. How do you rank them well without scoring all 100K expensively?

concepts.definition: Cheap per-shard candidate scoring, then expensive re-rank of the merged top candidates.
concepts.whyItMatters: Spends ranking cost only where it matters, keeping latency bounded.
concepts.example: BM25 to get top-1000, then a learned model to order the final 10.

concepts.definition: A precomputed per-doc authority score (e.g. PageRank) independent of the query.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adds a strong relevance signal as a cheap lookup.
concepts.example: Blend BM25 with PageRank at re-rank time.

whyNow: Ranking is what makes results useful; two-phase ranking is the standard way to combine quality and latency.

recap.after: Two-phase ranked results blending text + authority.
recap.newRisk: At 100K QPS even cheap scatter-gather is heavy — need caching and freshness handling.

steps.title: 5. Caching for QPS and Latency
description: Query popularity is heavily skewed: a small set of queries recurs constantly. Cache full results for popular queries so repeats skip scatter-gather entirely.
Cache hot document metadata/snippets so result hydration doesn't refetch per query. Both caches cut both latency and load.
Bound cache staleness with TTLs so fresh content still surfaces; invalidate or expire result-cache entries when the index for those terms updates materially.
steps.decisionPrompt: What fraction of queries are repeats, and how does that change your capacity plan?
whyNow: Caching is how the QPS target is actually met affordably; recognizing query skew is the practical insight.

concepts.definition: Caching the full ranked result for a (popular) query string.
concepts.whyItMatters: Repeated queries skip the entire scatter-gather + rank path.
concepts.example: Top trending queries served from cache with a short TTL.

recap.after: Popular queries and hot docs served from cache.
recap.newRisk: Stale results vs freshness; the index must keep updating as the crawl finds new content.

steps.title: 6. Freshness and Continuous Indexing
description: The crawl never stops, so the index must update continuously. Indexing produces immutable segments that are added and periodically merged; serving reads the current segment set, so new content appears without rebuilding the whole index.
Prioritize freshness in the crawl frontier (re-crawl fast-changing or important pages sooner) and propagate new segments to shards quickly so breaking content is searchable within a bounded time.
Recompute query-independent scores (link graph / PageRank) periodically as the graph evolves; serve with the latest available scores.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does a brand-new news article become searchable minutes after publication?

concepts.definition: Build small immutable index segments, add them live, merge in the background.
concepts.whyItMatters: New content is searchable without rebuilding the full index.
concepts.example: Fresh docs -> new segment -> shard serves it immediately; merged later.

whyNow: Closing on freshness shows search is a living pipeline, not a one-time build — tying crawl priority and incremental indexing to the freshness requirement.

recap.newRisk: Segment merge cost and score-recompute lag — bounded by background merging and incremental updates.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A replica serves it; the shard can be rebuilt from the document store / segments.
failureDrills.mitigation: Shard replication + rebuildable index from durable docs.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Web Search Engine
finalDesign.description: A crawler fleet pulls from a politeness-aware URL frontier, dedups by URL/content, and stores raw pages. An indexing pipeline parses docs into a document-partitioned inverted index (immutable, merged segments) and builds a link graph for query-independent page scores. The query service checks a result cache, otherwise scatters the query to all (replicated) shards, gathers per-shard top-k candidates, and a ranker re-ranks the merged set blending text relevance with page authority and freshness; snippets hydrate from a doc cache. Popular queries and hot docs are cached; the crawl + segment-based indexing run continuously so new content is searchable within a bounded time.

nonFunctional.how: Parallel scatter-gather + caching + bounded per-shard work.
Per-host rate limits + robots in the frontier.

say: Split the problem into crawl, index, and serve.
Pin sub-second latency over billions of docs and high QPS.
Note politeness and freshness as real constraints.
Crawler + URL frontier feeding a document store.
Inverted index built by an indexing pipeline.
Document vs term partitioning and scatter-gather tail latency.
Two-phase ranking blending text relevance and page authority.
Caching for QPS; continuous segment-based indexing for freshness.
Tradeoffs: partitioning scheme, ranking cost vs latency, freshness vs cache staleness.
Mention spam/quality and personalization as extensions.

expectations: Crawls and builds an inverted index.
May miss sharding, scatter-gather, and ranking depth.
Reasons about partitioning tradeoffs and tail latency (hedging).
Designs incremental segment indexing for freshness.
Discusses ranking signal blending and caching under query skew precisely.

followUps: Document partitioning vs term partitioning — when would term partitioning win?
How do you control scatter-gather tail latency across thousands of shards?
How would you detect and demote web spam / low-quality pages?
How do you incorporate personalization or query understanding (synonyms, intent)?
How would you make a brand-new page searchable within seconds, not minutes?
